title: Malted seeded loaf
subtitle: A dark, slightly sweet tin loaf with a mixed-seed crust
excerpt: A brown tin loaf built with malted wheat flour and a blend of seeds inside the crumb and on top: sunflower, pumpkin, linseed, and sesame. The malt extract gives depth and a light sweetness without richness.
sourceNotes: Malted and seeded loaves have been part of British artisan baking since the mid-twentieth century. The formula here follows the classic approach of combining malted wheat flour with a proportion of strong white and adding malt extract for colour and flavour, as documented in UK breadmaking guides from the 1950s onwards. The seed blend follows the standard British bakery pattern.

content.text: does two things in this loaf: it feeds the yeast in the early proving stages and it gives the crumb its dark colour and the slight sweetness that makes malted bread work well with sharp cheddar or cold-roast-beef sandwiches. Do not substitute golden syrup or treacle, the flavour is different and the colour wrong.

Combine both flours, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Dissolve the malt extract in the lukewarm water and add it to the flour along with the oil and 40 g of the seeds. Mix to a rough dough, then turn out and knead for 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. The dough will be softer and stickier than a white loaf; resist adding too much extra flour.

Return the dough to the bowl, cover, and leave at room temperature for 1 hour until well risen. The malt extract will speed the fermentation slightly; in a warm kitchen (24°C+) the dough may be ready in 50 minutes.

Lightly oil a 900 g loaf tin. Knock back the dough, shape into an oblong, and place seam-side down in the tin. Brush the top with water and scatter the remaining 20 g of seeds over the surface, pressing them in lightly. Cover and prove for 35 to 45 minutes until the dough has risen about 2 cm above the rim of the tin.

Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional with a tray of water on the floor. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes; the loaf is done when the top is deep golden-brown and the seeds are toasted, and an internal probe reads 96°C. The loaf should sound hollow when tapped on the base. Cool on a wire rack for at least 45 minutes before slicing. Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more; an oven thermometer gives a reliable reading.
